title: Orangutans Seek Out Medicinal Plants
gist: A Scientific Reports study of ~100 wild Bornean orangutans over 20 years finds they non-randomly consume medicinal plants — about 30% of the 202 plant species eaten overlap with those used pharmacologically by Indigenous Dayak communities — extending earlier evidence of orangutan self-medication.

insights.claim: Across 12,236 recorded feeding events spanning 202 plant species, about 30 percent were recognized as medicinal by Indigenous Dayak communities, and orangutan consumption of those medicinal plants was non-random.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Animal ethnopharmacology may flag bioactive plants of pharma interest; basic ecology with thin direct market read.
insights.quote: about 30 percent were recognized as medicinal plants by the Indigenous Dayak communities of the region
insights.locator: A total of 12,236 feeding events were recorded

insights.claim: An earlier 2024 observation of a Sumatran orangutan named Raku treating a facial wound with chewed Fibraurea tinctoria leaves — a plant with analgesic, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties — is now shown to be part of a broader suite of orangutan behaviors.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Strengthens evidence that great apes deliberately use plant medicine.
insights.quote: Raku selected leaves from a plant vine, Fibraurea tinctoria, known to have medicinal properties
